lens.included_activities: Independent US aluminum, titanium, nickel, copper, magnesium, and other nonferrous forging firms in the approximately $1-10 million normalized EBITDA band repeatedly producing qualified forged components for external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive forging departments, iron and steel forgers, nonferrous mills without outsourced forging programs, firms manufacturing primarily proprietary finished products, shells, non-control financing vehicles, and firms outside the EBITDA band.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Recurring engineered business-to-business programs priced per part, weight, or lot with tooling, setup, heat treatment, testing, machining or finishing, alloy surcharges, purchase orders, releases, and multiyear qualification or supply agreements.

a.bridge: BLS measures forging-machine occupations across metals and industries rather than wage-weighted tasks in 332112. Heating, manipulation, press or hammer operation, die work, trimming, heat treatment, inspection, maintenance, and handling were treated as largely physical, while estimating, engineering-document support, scheduling, quality analysis, maintenance knowledge, procurement, and administration were partly exposed; aerospace-heavy nonferrous shops may have more engineering and documentation than the proxy.
a.rationale: Nonferrous forging relies on heavy equipment, close temperature control, dies, skilled setup, manipulation, heat treatment, testing, and traceability. AI can assist the surrounding information workflows but cannot replace most plant-floor wages without new physical automation.
a.change_evidence: A payroll-weighted task census from independent nonferrous forgers by alloy, process, and end market, documenting current presses, manipulators, furnaces, inspection, ERP, simulation, and engineering automation.
caveats: Occupation evidence is national and not specific to 332112.
Highly regulated aerospace and defense programs can have unusually high documentation labor but also unusually low substitutability.

rho.rationale: Quote retrieval, work-instruction drafts, schedule and furnace optimization, quality trend analysis, maintenance support, procurement, and administration are implementable over five years. Sparse failures, customer validation, export controls, cybersecurity, alloy-specific metallurgy, traceability, and mandatory human approvals constrain realization.
rho.change_evidence: Audited nonferrous-forging deployments reporting labor hours, quote time, yield, energy, downtime, first-pass quality, false defect calls, customer requalification, and human-review rates before and after AI use.
caveats: Digital-twin research demonstrates technical potential, not economical LMM implementation.
Existing simulation and process control must be separated from new AI benefit.

e.rationale: Independent nonferrous forgers generally provide recurring, qualified external manufacturing and fit the operating lens. Captive aerospace or industrial units, proprietary finished-product producers, customer concentration, obsolete equipment, export or certification gaps, environmental exposure, weak management depth, and size-band errors remove a substantial minority.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the frozen n population showing captive status, external revenue, alloy, forging process, end market, normalized EBITDA, customer concentration, equipment, certifications, tooling ownership, environmental liabilities, and stand-alone management.
caveats: No observed eligible-firm denominator was found.
The injected n=25 uses a machinery margin bridge and is so small that a few misclassifications materially change the pool.

s5.bridge: Census reports owner ages across responding employer businesses rather than control transfers for nonferrous forgers. A portion of the 51% age-55-plus population plus ordinary strategic sales is translated into five-year qualifying transfers after excluding internal succession, continued ownership, closures, and nontransferable firms; the tiny buyer and seller pool may make this overstate regular transfer, while scarce qualified capacity may make it understate strategic purchases.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership creates succession pressure and qualified nonferrous capacity can have strategic value. The very small estimated population, high capital needs, customer approvals, export controls, environmental diligence, and key-person risk reduce completed-transfer probability.
s5.change_evidence: A ten-year census of completed, failed, and withdrawn US nonferrous-forging transactions in the EBITDA band, paired with owner-age, succession, customer-consent, export-control, environmental, and capital-plan interviews.
caveats: Owner-age evidence is all-industry and data year 2018.
Sparse transactions make any annualized transfer estimate unstable.

q.rationale: Alloy expertise, qualifications, tooling, traceability, scarce capacity, and high switching costs can protect gains from yield, energy, uptime, lead time, and quality. Metal surcharges, open-book programs, negotiated productivity sharing, cost-down demands, and rebids still return some value to customers.
q.change_evidence: A representative contract and program-margin sample showing alloy and energy pass-through, open-book costing, cost-down clauses, customer qualification, tooling ownership, renewal cadence, and margin persistence after productivity improvements.
caveats: No contract-level retention dataset was found.
Retention is likely higher in scarce aerospace and defense qualifications than in automotive or general industrial programs.

d5.bridge: BLS projects real output for all fabricated metal products, not nonferrous forged quantity, to grow 0.8% annually through 2034; USGS reports aluminum use rather than forgings. Applying modest five-year growth may understate aerospace, defense, lightweighting, and reshoring or overstate demand where alloy costs, imports, casting, machining, or additive processes substitute.
d5.rationale: BLS expects fabricated-metal real output growth, and USGS reports transportation accounted for 36% of US aluminum consumption in 2024. Lightweight transportation and qualified aerospace or defense applications support growth, while forging-machine employment declines, alloy costs, imports, and process substitution temper the case.
d5.change_evidence: Five-year North American nonferrous-forging shipment forecasts by alloy, process, and aerospace, defense, automotive, energy, and industrial end market, reconciled with program awards, backlogs, imports, substitution, and capacity additions.
caveats: Transportation aluminum consumption includes sheet, extrusions, castings, and other products, not just forgings.
Broad fabricated-metal output can diverge sharply from this tiny qualified segment.

o.rationale: Qualified nonferrous forgings require accountable billet and alloy control, heating, deformation, dies, heat treatment, testing, traceability, and delivery. Software and automated equipment can improve tasks, but customers still require a responsible manufacturing operator within five years.
o.change_evidence: Evidence of customers broadly internalizing forging, alternative processes eliminating forged components, or autonomous nonferrous forging plants without accountable metallurgy, production, maintenance, and quality management.
caveats: Process substitution is captured primarily in d5.
Digital ordering and reporting can become self-service without eliminating the physical operator.

narrative.executive_view: Nonferrous forging offers durable, highly qualified physical programs and potentially strong retention, but the frozen LMM universe is only 25 estimated firms. AI is a support layer for quoting, engineering records, quality, maintenance, scheduling, and administration rather than a substitute for the forging operation.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can retrieve analogous jobs, assist estimates and instructions, optimize heating and schedules, summarize inspection trends, prioritize maintenance, and draft records. Billet handling, heating, press or hammer operation, die work, heat treatment, testing release, and repair remain physical or human-supervised.
narrative.value_capture: Scarce alloys, qualifications, tooling, traceability, and switching costs help retain yield, energy, delivery, and reliability gains. Metal pass-through, open-book costing, cost-down provisions, and productivity sharing return a portion to customers.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen estimate of 25 band firms is exceptionally small and depends on a machinery margin bridge. Captive status, end-market qualifications, export controls, equipment, customer concentration, management, and environmental liabilities determine the already narrow transferable pool.
narrative.demand_durability: Aerospace, defense, lightweight transportation, and specialized industrial applications support modest growth, while imports, alloy prices, cycles, and alternative processes create downside. Broad data are positive but only indirect for this small segment.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: Key gaps are six-digit tasks and shipments, alloy and end-market mix, current automation, program backlogs, customer contracts, export controls, equipment, environmental liabilities, and true EBITDA. One program loss or seller misclassification can materially change the opportunity.
narrative.principal_driver: Scarce qualified nonferrous capacity and switching costs support durable demand and retention of yield, quality, and reliability improvements.
narrative.principal_weakness: The transferable population is exceptionally small, while most labor remains physical and customer qualification constrains implementation.

sources.title: NAICS 332112: Nonferrous Forging
sources.supports: Census identifies 332112 as nonferrous forging within fabricated metal product manufacturing and distinguishes it from iron and steel forging and adjacent forming processes.

sources.title: Forging Machine Setters, Operators, and Tenders, May 2023
sources.supports: BLS reports 9,170 forging-machine setters, operators, and tenders nationally in 2023 and defines their work as setting up, operating, or tending machines that shape parts.

sources.title: Occupational projections and worker characteristics, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS reports 8,800 forging-machine setters, operators, and tenders in 2024 and projects 7,200 in 2034, an 18.9% decline.

sources.title: Using Deep Reinforcement Learning for Zero Defect Smart Forging
sources.supports: The research develops a digital-twin optimization strategy for induction-heating control in a forging line using pyrometer temperature data, supporting technical feasibility while not establishing LMM economics.

sources.title: Aluminum Forming Effluent Guidelines
sources.supports: EPA identifies NAICS 332112 within the aluminum-forming effluent-guideline scope, supporting environmental process controls and accountable operation.

sources.title: Business Owners' Ages
sources.supports: The 2019 Annual Business Survey infographic reports 51% of responding employer-business owners were age 55 or older; estimates cover 4.1 million responding owners in data year 2018.

sources.title: USGS Mineral Commodity Summaries 2025: Aluminum
sources.supports: USGS reports that transportation applications accounted for 36% of US aluminum consumption in 2024 and that primary aluminum production value was about $1.9 billion.

sources.title: Employment and output by industry, 2024-2034
sources.supports: BLS projects fabricated-metal-product real output increasing from $303.5 billion in 2024 to $329.5 billion in 2034, a 0.8% annual rate, while employment declines 3.9%.
